title: Alcaraz's French Open Threepeat Hopes Crushed by Wrist Injury
summary:
Carlos Alcaraz's quest for a third consecutive French Open title has come to an abrupt halt. The 22-year-old Spanish tennis sensation announced on Friday that he will not compete at Roland Garros this year due to a right wrist injury, ending his clay season prematurely.

Alcaraz revealed the decision on social media, stating that tests on his right wrist led him and his team to conclude that caution was the best path forward. "We have decided that the most prudent thing to do is to be cautious and not participate in Rome or Roland Garros," he wrote. "This is a difficult time for me, but I'm sure we will come out of it stronger."

The injury occurred during the first round of the Barcelona Open last week, where Alcaraz beat Otto Virtanen but subsequently withdrew from the tournament. He had already pulled out of the Madrid Masters on April 17, raising concerns about his availability for the French Open.

The timing is especially tough for Alcaraz, who just completed the career Grand Slam in January by winning the Australian Open, becoming the youngest man ever to achieve that feat. He holds a 22-3 record this season and won a title in Doha. But after losing the world No. 1 ranking to Jannik Sinner following the Monte Carlo Masters final on April 12, this setback pushes him further from the top spot.

Sinner, who has never won the French Open, expressed sympathy for his rival. "It's sad news for all of us, me being a competitor you want to play against the best players in the world, and he's definitely the best player on this surface," Sinner said after a win at the Madrid Open. "Being that young like he is and like I am, we need to look at our bodies first before worse things [happen]."

Alcaraz had hinted earlier in the week that he would prioritize his long-term health. "I'd rather come back maybe a bit later, but in great shape, than come back quickly and risk making this injury worse," he explained. "I have a long career ahead of me, so I'm not afraid to miss what I have to miss."

This will be just the second Grand Slam Alcaraz has missed since his main draw debut in 2021. The French Open runs from May 24 to June 7, and fans will now watch to see if Sinner can capitalize on Alcaraz's absence.
why_it_matters: This story matters to 12-14 year olds because it illustrates the tough choices elite athletes make between short-term glory and long-term health. It also shows how injuries can shift the balance in sports, affecting rankings and rivalries. Understanding these dynamics gives you insight into the pressures of professional sports and the importance of resilience.

keywords.explanation: The 22-year-old Spanish tennis star who withdrew from the French Open due to a right wrist injury.
The clay-court Grand Slam tournament also known as Roland Garros; Alcaraz won the last two editions.

Alternate name for the French Open where Alcaraz had hoped to compete.

background_read: Carlos Alcaraz burst onto the scene as a teenager and by age 22 had already won four Grand Slam titles. He completed the career Grand Slam at the 2025 Australian Open, becoming the youngest man to do so, surpassing Rafael Nadal's mark. The career Grand Slam means winning all four majors at least once.

The French Open is played on clay, a surface that requires sliding and strong wrist control. Injuries to the wrist are particularly damaging for clay-court players. Alcaraz had previously missed Wimbledon in 2022 due to a similar injury.

The men's tour has seen a rivalry develop between Alcaraz and Jannik Sinner. Sinner, also 22, took over the world No. 1 ranking in April 2025 after winning the Monte Carlo Masters, beating Alcaraz in the final. Their matches are often considered the future of tennis. Missing a Grand Slam can affect rankings and momentum, but players often prioritize long-term health.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Alcaraz's quest for a third consecutive French Open title ends with injury announcement.
└─ Quote: 'The most prudent thing to do is to be cautious.'
├─ Right wrist injury occurred during Barcelona Open first round.
└─ Also pulled out of Madrid Masters on April 17.
├─ Had just completed career Grand Slam at Australian Open in January.
├─ Lost No. 1 ranking to Sinner after Monte Carlo final on April 12.
└─ This push pushes him further from top spot.
RIVAL'S RESPONSE: Jannik Sinner expresses sympathy, calls Alcaraz 'best player on this surface'.
├─ Quote: 'I'd rather come back maybe a bit later, but in great shape.'
CONTEXT: Only second Grand Slam missed since main draw debut in 2021.
OPEN QUESTION: Can Sinner capitalize on Alcaraz's absence at the French Open?

perspectives.description: Alcaraz made a mature decision to prioritize his health over a tournament. This shows wisdom beyond his years and will likely extend his career. Fans appreciate that he's thinking long-term.

Tennis fans and organizers are disappointed because Alcaraz’s absence diminishes the excitement of the French Open. A threepeat would have been historic. His injury also hurts the rivalry with Sinner, as fans won't get to see them battle on clay.

This injury is a reminder of how physically demanding tennis is. Alcaraz's aggressive style, which relies on power and wrist snap, may make him more prone to such injuries. Data shows that many young stars face similar setbacks (e.g., Bjorn Borg retired early, Rafael Nadal had many injuries).

If Alcaraz recovers fully by Wimbledon (July), he could still challenge for the title there. Meanwhile, Sinner has a golden opportunity to win his first French Open. The rivalry might become even more intense when Alcaraz returns.
